profile.name: Sam Alvarez
profile.business_type: Small-batch hot sauce — launching in 3 months

goal: Pick a brand name — I've narrowed to two and I can't decide

constraints.budget: name matters more than budget right now — but I have $3K for initial branding total
constraints.time: labels need to be designed in 3-4 weeks

personality: decisive elsewhere, stuck on this specific decision; will resist a rushed answer and will reject either name if the reasoning feels generic
backstory: Spent two years developing recipes for small-batch hot sauces with an unusual flavor profile — fermented fruit (peach, plum) + mild smoke + moderate heat. Not a heat-warrior brand, not a gourmet-only brand. The sauces are medium-heat with depth. She has narrowed the brand name to two candidates: 'Smolder' (evocative of the smoke + warmth, intentionally ambiguous about heat) and 'Glacier Falls' (her grandfather's ranch name, evocative of place + calm + cold, intentionally counter-positioning against the heat-warrior category). Both have pros. Both have the fatal flaw of being wrong for the product in different ways she can't quite articulate. She's been asking friends and family; everyone has an opinion and none of them help. She's not asking about channels or pricing — she's asking about the angle the name carries and whether either of these commits to the right one.
conversational_behaviors: Will not move the conversation to marketing channels, pricing, or distribution — the brand name is the question
Will react to each option with a specific feeling ('Smolder feels like it's trying too hard' or 'Glacier Falls doesn't sound like a hot sauce at all') and wait for the panel to engage that feeling
Will reject a generic 'pick the name that tells your story' framing immediately

expected_answer_style.opening: Opens with the two names, the flavor profile, and the exact phrasing 'I can't decide' — asking about the angle each name carries
expected_answer_style.followup: Engages best with commit-and-exclude framing — 'Smolder commits to heat-adjacent while Glacier Falls commits to flavor-over-heat; which story does the product actually tell?'
